text: ADELNAU, a town and circle of Prussia, in the prov. of Posen, on the I. bank of the Bartsch, 20 m. SW of Kalisch. Pop. of the town 1,600. The circle of A. is a marshy wooded district. Pop. in 1840, 52,250.
ADELPHI, or Fratelli, four uninhabited islets in the Grecian archipelago, to the S of Kelidromi, in N lat. 39° 6', E long. 23° 59'. Alt. 581 ft.

ADELSBERG, a circle and a market-town of Illyria, in the gov. of Laibach, 21 m. SW of Laibach. The town stands on an extensive plain at the foot of a long range of hills. Pop. 1,400. It is "a poor place and apparently little frequented" [Gleig], but is celebrated for its extensive caverns, one of which has been explored to a distance of above 3 m. from ADE

ADELAIDE ISLAND, the westernmost of a its entrance which is about a mile from the town, at a point where the river Poik suddenly sinks under ground amidst a group of sterile limestone hills, to re-appear, it is said, as the Unz at Planina. A good account of this grotto will be found in the New Monthly Magazine for July 1830. About 3 m. from A. is another grotto, known as that of the Magdalene, in which runs a small stream well-known to naturalists as one of the few localities in which the Proteus anguinus, the connecting link between the fish and the reptile, is found.—Kuttner.—Sir H. Davy.—Rev. G. R. Gleig.
ADELSHAUSEN, a town of Baden, in the circle of the Upper Rhine. Pop. 332.
ADELSHEIM, a town and bailiwick of Baden, in the circle of the Lower Rhine, 18 m. ENE of Neckar-Elz. Pop. of the b. 12,139; of the t. 1,488.
ADELSHOFEN, a town of Baden, in the circle of the Middle Rhine. Pop. 580.
ADEMUZ, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 55 m. NW of Valentia, on the Turia or Guadalaviar.

ADEN, a town of Yemen, in the Abdali territory, near the mouth of the Red sea; the Athana of the Greeks and Romans, and probably the Eudaimon of Arrian, and Arabia Emporion of Ptolemy. It is in 12° 46' 10" N lat., and 45° 5' E long. From Bombay its distance is 1,641 m. W by S; and from Suez, by Mocha and Jidda, it is distant 1,295 m. [Chesney.] By water it is 100 m. distant from the entrance of the Red sea, opposite Perím, or Mayún island, and about 145 m. from Mocha; by land, the distance between Aden and Mocha diminishes, being only 120 m., or three days' journey on a Behüri camel, or one trained for riding; but this route is dangerous, owing to the savage temper of the Subbeihi Bedouins, to whom the greater part of the intervening territory belongs. The peninsula of A. may be generally described as a high rocky promontory, "a congeries of dark and sombre rocks" [Dr. Wilson], rising in Jebel Shamshan to an elevation of 1,776 ft. above the sea; <math>5\frac{1}{4}</math> m. in length, from Rás Tarshein on the W, to Rás Marshigh on the E, and varying in breadth from <math>2\frac{1}{4}</math> to <math>3\frac{1}{2}</math> m.; connected to the mainland by a sandy isthmus about 3 m. in length, which, at high water, is 1,350 yards in breadth. On the eastern side, and open to the sea, is a plain 3 m. in circumference, upon which stands the present town, built upon the ruins of the former one, "ensconced in an amphitheatre of rocky mountains, literally in the crater of a volcano" [Wilson], the bottom of which is nearly flat, and little above sea-level. The houses or huts are of slight construction, many of them entirely of wicker-work, with waggon-roofs covered with the leaves of the date-palm. Opposite to and commanding A. is Sirah island, a triangular rock 1,200 yards long by 700 broad, and 430 ft. high, running nearly N and S. On its northern point, where boats unload, there was formerly a pier, but it is now in ruins; there is a tower, having a magazine underneath, immediately above it, and gently rising from the tower a piece of ground extends 380 ft. long by 60 to 150 broad. At low water this island joins A., but tradition asserts that a deep channel once existed between them. On its southern extremity it approaches within 80 yards of a rock, which juts out from the side of the town and is precipitous. Mr. Burr, in a paper read before the Geological society, supposes the crater in which A. is built to have subsided, and that the former range of the face is indicated by this abrupt island. The crater has also been cleft through from N to S; and the rents thus produced in its walls are called the Northern and Southern passes. From the circumstance of A. being on a peninsula, it has two bays, the one on the eastern or town-side, and the other on the western. The latter is at its entrance 3\frac{1}{2} m. broad, and runs up to a distance of 7 m.; the anchorage in it is excellent and of great extent; steamers may here run in during the night, and load and unload at all seasons in perfect security, and that too close to the shore. The best place for ships to lie in has 5 to 6 fathoms water. The tidal rise, at full and change, is 81 ft. The land which forms the continental side of the West or Back bay has a very picturesque appearance, and its peaks rise to a great height. During the prevalence of the easterly winds ships usually prefer this baythat is, from September to June. The anchorage in East bay is likewise good and very regular, varying from 5 to 10 fathoms. A heavy swell runs in during the NE monsoon, but from June to the end of August smooth water is found close to Sirah island; during those months the shumal blows with incredible violence from the W.—In the subjoined sketch, A is the western bay; b, Ras Tarshein; c, Ras Sinailah; d, Ras Marshigh; E, Sirah isle; F, Jebel Shamshan; g g, the line of new fortifications across the isthmus; and h h, remains of an aqueduct. 人一 The British government, in consequence of an act of piracy committed on a vessel from Madras, in 1837, took possession of this place in 1839, but agreed to pay the sultan of Lahidge an annual sum of 8,700 German crowns for it in perpetuity. In 1802, Sir Home Popham, while on a mission from Bengal to Arabia, visited A., and was the first to call the attention of the Indian government to its advantages both in a commercial and political point of view. The Bombay government supposed, and with justice, that, besides forming an execllent half-way house between Bombay and Suez, and being singularly adapted from local circumstances as a depôt for coals, A. might under British protection attain somewhat of its former commercial celebrity. They well knew that it was once the chief mart of Arabia, and they indulged a hope that it might again become the port-of-export for the coffee, gums, and spices of that country, and the channel through which the produce of India and England might be spread through the rich provinces of Yemen and Hadramaut. Another thing which struck them was the advantage which it possesses over Mocha-ingress and egress being at all times easy for ships, whereas the contrary exists in the Red sea. Vessels from Burburra, on the Sumali coast opposite A., with gums, olives, frankincense, elephants' teeth, &c, can run across in a day in the NE monsoon, and return in another; it is as near the coffee-districts as Mocha; and Sana is quite as close. A. is moreover celebrated for the goodness of its water: being the only part of the Arabian coast on which that important article is to be obtained of good quality. It is drawn from land-springs as well as from the ravines and gullies. The possession of A. was long eagerly contended for by two great powers, the Turks and the Portuguese, struggling for mastery in the East; and when no longer able to maintain their rivalry, it reverted into the hands of its ancient masters, the Arabs; and in 1708 was governed by its own sheik. The security afforded by its natural defences, aided by the fortifications—the work of former times-rendered it a suitable retreat for the piratical hordes of the Desert. The lawless sons of Ishmael ADE could from this stronghold rush out upon the adjacent waters, and make themselves masters of the wealth of those adventurers who dared to encounter the dangers of the Red sea. In the 11th, 12th, and 13th cent., A. maintained an extensive trade with India and China. But immediately after the discovery of the passage to India round the Cape, it fell into decay; and previous to the occupation of the British, the pop. of Aden scarcely exceeded 600 souls. In December 1842 it amounted to 16,454, exclusive of the military, and their camp-followers, who numbered 3,484 in all. Of this pop. 12,170 were Arabs, 2,050 Sumális, and 1,070 Jews. The Europeans, male and female, including the troops, were 857. The trade of A., before we obtained possession, was very trifling, the imports consisting of a few English cotton-cloths, with lead, iron, and tin-which were brought by Baglás on their way to Mocha—rice, dates, and a few cattle; while the exports were limited to a little coffee, millet, and a few drugs.—The whole coast of Southern Arabia, from the straits of Bab-el-Mandeb to Hadramaut, is sometimes known as the Beled-Aden or country of Aden.—Salt's Travels.—Milburn's Oriental Commerce.—Niebuhr's Voyage.—Parl. Report on Steam Navigation to India, 1834. — Wellsted's Arabia. — Haines's Memoir of the S and E Coasts of Arabia. Dr. Wilson's Lands of the Bible.

ADEN (CAPE), the southern point of Jebel Shamshán, mentioned in the above article. It is sometimes known as Rás Sinaïlah, and is 2 m. W of Rás Marshigh. Captain Haines fixes its position in N lat. 12° 45', E long. 45° 09', or 45° 3' Raper.

ADEN (GULF OF), a name sometimes given to that portion of the Arabian sea which lies between the meridians of Cape Guardafui and Bab-el-Mandeb, presenting an area which may approximate to 90,000 sq. m. The N coast of this gulf—which is sometimes known as the gulf of Bab-el-Mandeb, and sometimes as that of Socotra-may be regarded as commencing at the prominent cape of Rás Farták, in N lat. 15° 37', E. long. 52° 16', the SW extremity of a deep bay, and formed by a mountain 2,500 ft. high. The shore between this point and Rás Derkah, in N lat. 15° 27', E long. 51° 50′, is low, and studded with small villages. To the W of Rás Derkah is the low sandy bay of Keshin, with good holding-ground in 6 or 10 fathoms, but exposed to the NE monsoon. The coast is here quite barren, with a range of dark-coloured hills on the background. The W side of the bay of Keshín is formed by Rás Sharwein. Passing a series of small sandy bays, we arrive at Rás Aghrib or Akáb, between which and Misenát, on a low dreary coast, is the town of Sihút, in N lat. 15° 13', E long. 51° 13'. About 13 m. W by S from Misenat is Raidah; and 30 m. beyond Raidah is Ras Kosair. At Ras Baghashú, in N lat. 14° 49', E long. 50° 4', a series of bold limestone cliffs commence which run on nearly due W for 8 m. to Rás Sharmah. Between this last cape and Hami, 9 m. farther W, the coast forms a bay 2 m. deep with a sandy bottom, and affording good shelter during the NE monsoon. Proceeding eastwards 17 m. we arrive at the town of Shehr, off which is a good open roadstead. Passing along the coast trending SW in an unbroken line of low sand, with soundings 2 m. off shore, we arrive at Ras Makallah, a low neck of land projecting about 2 m. in a SSE direction from the base of the hills which here come down close to the shore. The town of Makallah is in N lat. 14° 31′, E long. 49° 6′. A low barren sombre coast composed of sand-hills, which appear to stretch inland for some miles, lies between Makallah and Rás Makdahah 53 m. farther SW. Separated by a channel about a mile in width from the last-mentioned cape, with 15 fathoms water, is Baraghah island, to the W of which is another small island called Sikkah or Jibris. Both these islets are lofty and precipitous. The coast from this point continues in a W by S direction to Rás-el-Asídah, the E cape of Ghubbet Ain, in N lat. 13° 57', E long. 48° 9'. The bay of Ghubbet Ain is 6 m. deep, with a clean bottom and regular soundings, but open to the W. The tide rises in it from 5 to 6 ft. The W extremity of this bay is formed by Rás-al-Kosaïr, a low rounded point. About 12 m. to the SW is the village of Haura, 1 m. SW of Rás Safwán, the extremity of the Jebel Hamari, which extends about 30 m. in a NE direction, and in its highest central peak attains an elevation of 5,284 ft. To Ras Urlajah the coast is flat, with 160 fathoms at 6 m. distance. From Cape Makátein in N lat. 13° 24', E long. 46° 26', which shelters a good anchorage, to Sughrá, a distance of 44 m., the coast is irregular, and presents a lofty range of mountains about 5 m. inland. Sughrá is in N lat. 13° 22', E long. 45° 40'. A reef lies off it at the distance of half-a-mile, with 9 fathoms water 600 yards from its edge. From Sughrá to Seilán, a distance of l 20 m., the coast runs SSW, with 20 fathoms soundings at 5 m. off. The bay of Ghubbet Seilán has flat sandy shores, with shoal water to a considerable distance off shore. From its W extremity, the coast trends suddenly SSW for 19 m., to Cape Aden or Rás Sinailah in N lat. 12° 45', E long. 45° 3'. The W headland of Aden West bay is formed by Jebel Hasan, a peninsular granite promontory 6 m. long from E to W by 3 m. in breadth, and rising 1,237 ft. above sea-level. Between Jebel Hasan and Rás Amrán is a bay 5 m. broad by 2 deep, called Bander Feikam; and between Ras Amrán and Ras Kau is another bay of nearly equal extent bordered by a flat and sandy coast. From Ras Kau, or the Black cape, in N lat. 12° 40', E long. 44° 27', to the entrance of Khór Amrán, a remarkable land-locked islet, is a distance of 17 m. W by S. A dangerous bank stretches, 31 m. off shore, from Khór Amrán to Rás Arah the S extremity of Arabia at the entrance of the strait of Bab-el-Mandeb, in N lat. 12° 38′, E long. 43° 56′, 13\frac{1}{2} m. W by S from the Khor. From Ras Arah to Ras Bab-el-Mandeb the coast first runs WSW for 25 m., and then SW 7 m., showing a number of small projecting rocky points with a sandy beach. Thus far Captain Haines' survey of the South coast of Arabia has enabled us accurately to trace the northern coast of the gulf of Aden: our materials for describing the southern coast are much less satisfactory. Ras Seján, a cape on the Abessinian shore, forms the southern point of the shore of Bab-el-Mandeb, and is distant from the Arabian coast 141 geog. m. From Ras Seján to Ras-el-Bir the coast runs in a SSE direction about 20 m., and is low and sandy, rising in the interior into flat-topped limestone mountains. At Ras-el-Bir, the coast suddenly trends SW by W for a distance of 80 m., along the SE coast of Adel. Tajurrah, in N lat. 11° 21', presents a good roomy harbour; and to the WSW of Tajurrah, the gulf runs deeply inland, forming a natural boundary between the territory of the Danakil and that of the Somauli. From the S promontory of this inlet, the coast-line curves E by S, and then SE to Zeila, which has a bad harbour and shoal water for nearly half-amile from the beach. From Zeila to Berbera or Burbura, a distance of 120 m., the general direction of the coast is SE and then ESE. From Berbera, which is nearly on the meridian of Aden, the gulf being 170 m. wide between the two places—the coastline runs NE to Ras Karrum, which is nearly in N lat. 11°, E long. 46°. From Ras Karrúm to Ras Met in about N lat. 11° 48', E long. 50° 45', a distance of 350 m., the general direction of the coast is E by N, and the gulf gradually expands to a breadth, between Ras Met and the opposite coast, between Misenat ADE and Ras Kosair, of about 220 m. From Ras Met to Ras Asser, Aseïr, or Guardafui, in N lat. 11° 50', E long. 51° 22', which marks the S entrance of the gulf. and is the most eastern point of Africa, the coast trends round from ESE to SE; and the width of the entrance of the gulf between Cape Guardafui on the S and Ras Fartak, bearing NNE, on the opposite coast, is 275 m. The foregoing outline of the S coast of the gulf of A. is far from satisfactory either in the amount or precision of its details, because it embraces an extent of upwards of 600 m. of almost unknown coast, -the only portion indeed of the African coast which has not yet been subjected to nautical survey. Captain Haines states that during the months of January, February, and March, E and NE breezes prevail in the gulf of A., increasing from A. to the straits of Bab-el-Mandeb. In April and May, the winds are light with clear weather, and the thermometer ranges from 80° to 95°. June is an unsettled month. Fogs are frequent in July and August, and it often blows hard from W and SW. In September the W wind ceases, and calm sultry nights prevail. The NE monsoon reaches Makallah about Nov. 5. The SW monsoon blows out of the Red sea, and outside of the straits takes a W direction, but seldom extends far beyond Aden. At Cape Guardafui it blows with great violence along the coast from NNE, and thence across the gulf to Ras Rehmat.—The two coasts of the gulf have their own currents running in the direction of and varying with the monsoons; and the central part has others running in every direction except during strong breezes.—Captain Haines' Papers in Geog. Journ., vols. ix. and xv.—Raper.
ADENAR, an island in the Malay archipelago, separated from the eastern extremity of Floris by a very narrow channel. Its centre is in S lat. 8° 30', and E long. 123° 10'.

ADENAU, a circle and a town of Prussia in the prov. of the Rhine, and gov. of Coblentz. The pop. of the circle, which is a rugged mountainous district lying in the Eifel, was, in 1831, 21,640, of whom 21,561 were Catholics. The town of A. has a pop. of 1,330. It is upon the Adenau, a small tributary of the Ahr, 8 m. S by W of Altenahr, near the Hohe Acht, the highest summit of the Eifel mountains.—There are some linen and cotton manufactures in the town; and in the neighbourhood are mines of lead and of iron.
ADENDOUM, a town of Marocco, in the prov. of Temsna, in N lat. 33° 10', W long. 6° 34'. There is a considerable lake to the E of this town.
ADERBIJAN. See AZERDBIJAN.
ADERNO, a small town of Sicily, in the Val di Demone, 15 m. WNW of Catania, near the l. bank of the Giarretta or Simetto. It is on or near the site of the ancient Adranum.

ADERSBACH, a v. of Bohemia, in the circle of Königgrätz, on the borders of Silesia, and 19 m. SSE of Landshut. It is divided into Upper A. and Lower A. The pop. of the former is 568; of the latter, 602. The neighbourhood of A. is celebrated for its curious labyrinth of sandstone rocks - the Adersbacher Felsen—which are scattered in all directions over a district of country 6 m. in length by 3 m. in breadth. These rocks, which vary in altitude from 100 to 200 ft., have been compared to a gigantic stone forest.
A'DERSEG, a village of Abessinia, 2 hours NE of Zibákwaha, in the district of Ebenat.
ADERSLEBEN, a village of Prussia, on the Bode, in the circle and 6 m. E of Halberstadt. Pop. 400. Lat. 51° 54' N.
ADERVIELLE, a village of France, in the dep. of Hautes-Pyrénées, 20 m. SE of Bagnères-de-Bigorre. Pop. 200. ~
ADESSA, or ADEXE, a v. on the W coast of Teneriffe, opposite to the island of Gomera, which is distant 6 leagues W. The roadstead is open to the SW.—Malham.

ADGERUD, or AGEROOD, a fortified station on the road from Cairo to Suez, 123 m. from Suez. The Derb-el-Haj, or road of the pilgrim-caravan from Cairo to Mecca, strikes off from the Egyptian frontier at this place and runs E to Akaba. - Wilkinson.—Niebuhr.
ADGI'LLA, a town of Abessinia, in Gojam, near the Muga river, here about 20 ft. wide, and 2 ft. deep. A. was formerly a capital of Rás Gúksa.—Dr. Beke.
ADGI-SU, a river of Natolia, in the sanják of Kankari, which, after uniting with the Kara-Su, flows into the Kizil-Irmák.
ADGIUD, a town of Moldavia, 40 m. N of Fokshani, on the road from Bukharest to Czernovitz. Pop. 1,500.—Gaspari.
ADHARI. See HARRAR.
ADHEIM, a river of Persia, formed by the confluence of the Kerkúk, Torík, and Toz-Khurmetli, whose united waters it carries into the Tigris. Its general course is from NNE.

ADHIYA'RI, a branch or arm of the Indus, which leaves the parent stream a little below the Khediwari, and after pursuing a WSW course of 21 m., in which it is joined by the Musawari, enters the Khediwári. Where narrowest it has a breadth of 35 yards; its banks are steep; its depth at high water never less than 9 feet.—Lieut. Carless in Geog. Journ. vol. viii.

ADHRAA'T, or DRA'A', an Arab v. in Hauran. Near it 73 m. SSW from Nawa, is a large mound, called by the natives Tel-el-Ashtereh [i. e. 'the Hill of Ashtereh'] which Captain Newbold thinks may mark the site of the ancient Ashtaroth.—Geog. Journ. vol. xvi. p. 333.
ADIEUX (CAPE DES), on the coast of South Australia in S. lat. 31° 52′, E long. 132° 10′.

ADIGE, a large river of Tyrol and Venetian Lombardy. It has two head-branches, the Etsch, and the Eisach. The Etsch, the western branch, is formed by the union of two small mountain-streams, -the Rom or Rham, and the Etsch, which unite a little above Glurentz. The Rom rises at the foot of the Wurmser-Ioch, which separates it from the source of the Adda, and the valley of Bormio, at the lower part of the pass of Santa Maria, which is 8,200 ft. above the sea. It runs NE through the Munsterthal, passing the village of Santa Maria, 4,480 feet above the sea, and Munster. The Etsch rises at the head of the Matsheide, or valley of Mats; and runs first SW, crossing the great post-road which leads from Mats to the Upper Innthal. It then runs due S to join the Rom, at the bridge of Spandinig, a little below Glurentz. The bed of the Etsch-now called the Upper Adige—as it runs eastwards through the Vintschgau at the southern base of the Ortler-Spitz [12,855 ft.], is 3,076 ft. above sea-level. Near Meran it is joined by the Passeyr. From this junction the Upper Adige runs SE to near Botzen, below which it receives, or rather joins, the Eisach, descending from the Brenner, which is the larger and perhaps longer stream, and becomes navigable after this confluence. The Eisach takes its rise near the head of the pass of the Brenner [4,500 ft.]; runs SE to Brixen, where it receives the Rienz coming from the E from the foot of the Pentelstein pass [5,000 ft.]. From Brixen it runs SSW to Botzen, where it joins the Upper Adige. From Botzen the A. runs to the W of S through the longest transverse valley of the Alps, passing by Trent, and running parallel to the Lago di Garda from Roveredo to Pontone, having entered Venetian-Lombardy at Borghetto nearly 14 I. ADJ m. S of Roveredo. At Pontone it turns to the SE, a little above Verona, through which it flows; it then traverses Legnago, and turning eastwards at Badia, so as to run nearly parallel to the Po, falls into the Adriatic at Porto-Fossoni, 12 m. NW by N from Punta della Maestra, the outer point of the main stream of the Po, and 5 m. S of Chioggia, after a course of more than 250 m.—The A. receives a great number of tributary torrents and streams, amongst which are the Aviso, before it arrives at Trent; and the Alpon, between Legnago and Verona. The Nose is its principal affluent from the right. The canals of Castagnaro and Adigetto are supplied from the A. Its mean breadth, from Trent to Verona, is 300 to 330 ft.; but from Castagnaro to the sea it is double that breadth. Its depth varies from 6\frac{1}{2} to 13 ft. In winter its waters generally fall about 3 ft. In spring, from the melting of the snows, it is prodigiously increased, rising above its ordinary level sometimes to more than 16 ft., and inundating all the neighbouring plains. These inundations are particularly destructive at the Polesine de Rovigo, which would have been entirely ruined but for the canals and strong dykes which restrain its force. In 1757, Verona and its environs were inundated by the A. for 10 or 12 days. The A. is a very rapid stream, realizing the epithet of Claudian, 'velox Athesis,' its slope being from 20 to 25 ft. per mile. It is never frozen consequently but when the cold is excessive. It is navigable from Trent to the sea, but its navigation is difficult. At Badia it is connected with the Adigetto canal which joins that of Bianco at Retinella, and thus opens a communication with the Po. It does not abound in fish, but at its mouth is a fishery of sturgeons and salmon. Its waters are clear in the upper part of its course, but become gradually turbid as they descend. The valley of the A., from Trent to Verona, is about 3,280 feet in breadth. Below Verona it expands, and the adjacent lands are sometimes nearly 3 ft. below the level of the stream, which, in spite of the dykes, expose them to imminent risk from inundation.—The A. is memorable in the military annals both of ancient and modern times; and has given name to the campaign of fifteen days in 1796, in which Bonaparte disconcerted the plans of the Austrian general, Wurmser; defeated him at Salo, Lonato, and Castiglione; and forced him to retire into Tyrol.
ADINA' BAZAR, a small river of Persia, a branch of the Balahrúd, dividing part of the Russian frontier of Talisch from Ugarúd in Azerdbijan.

ADIRONDACK MOUNTAINS, a name recently given to a cluster of mountains on the NE of the state of New York, U. S., to the SW of Lake Champlain. They are connected with the Catskill range. Their highest summits are Whiteface, about 5,000 ft.; and Mount Marcy, 5,460 ft.
ADISHAM, a p. and v. in Kent. Area of p. 1,810 acres. Pop. 372. The v. is <math>2\frac{3}{4}</math> m. SW by S from Wingham.
ADISSA, a v. of Portugal, in Estremadura, near Almada. There is a gold mine in the neighbourhood of A., which in 1820 gave employment to 20 miners.

ADIYAMA'N, a town of Kurdistán, in N lat. 37° 46'. It is circularly disposed round a mound called the Castle, is surrounded by gardens and groves, and has several mosques. It contains 800 houses of Mahommedans, and 300 of Christians. Ainsworth estimates its elevation at 2,700 ft.
ADJA, a walled town of Yourriba, in N lat. 9° 6', E long. 5° 5', between Akkibosa and Loko.—Clapperton.

ADJEDEE, or WAD JEDI', a river of Algiers, which descends in several head-streams from the southern slope of the Atlas chain; flows E 300 m.; Sebkhat-el-Melghigh, in lat. 34° 26' N, long. 6° 30' E. -Carte de l'Algérie du Dep. Top. Paris, 1847.

ADJELU'N, or AJLU'N [Wilson], a v. of Syria, in about N lat. 32° 24', and 15 m. E of the Jordan, near the head of a stream which flows into the Jordan after a SW course of about 20 m. At an hour's distance to the NNW is the Callah-el-Rubbat, a hill which commands an "extensive view of the plain of the Jordan, the lakes Asphaltes and Tiberias, and a vast tract of country in every direction." [Irby and Mangles.] The surrounding country is mountainous and well-wooded. This place must not be confounded with the Ajalon of Scripture, which Dr. Robinson appears to have identified with Yalo 12 NW of Jerusalem.
ADJEMEER. See AJMEER.
ADJOTS (LES), a v. of France in Charente, 3 m. N of Ruffec. Pop. 896.
ADJYGHUR, or AJAY-GAR'H, a town of Hindostan, in Allahabad, in N lat. 24° 50', E long. 80° 3', celebrated for its strong fortress which was stormed by the British in 1809.
ADLER, or ERLITZ, a river of Bohemia, rising in the Sudeten, in two principal streams, and flowing into the Elbe on the l. bank at Königgrätz.

ADLERSBERG, or ARLBERG, a lofty summit at the western extremity of the secondary chain of the Tyrolese Alps which separates the valley of the Inn from that of the Lech. Its altitude is 10,200 ft. The pass of the A., leading from the valley of the Inn into the Vorarlberg [i. e. the country in front or to the W of the Arlberg], is one of the most frequented passes of the Alps, and may be considered as the western continuation of the road over the Brenner.
ADLER'S GRUND, a dangerous shoal in the Baltic sea, between the islands of Rugen and Bornholm, with only 14 ft. water. It is distant 33\frac{1}{2} m., E\frac{1}{2} S, from Orkona light.
ADLINGFLEET, a p. and v. in the W. R. of Yorkshire, near the junction of the Ouse and the Trent. The area of the p. is 4,580 acres. Pop. 448. The v. is 9 m. SE of Howden. Pop. 280.
ADLINGTON, a township and v. in the p. of Prestbury, Cheshire, 5 m. NW of Macclesfield. Pop. 1,159.—Also a township and v. in the p. of Standish, Lancashire, 4 m. N of Wigan. Pop. 1,130.
ADMINGTON, a hamlet in the p. of Quinton, Gloucestershire, 51 m. NNE of Chipping-Camden. Pop. 197.

ADMIRALTY BAY, a long inlet on the NW coast of Tavai Poenammoo, the middle island of the New Zealand group, between Cape Stephens and Point Lambert, opening into Cook's strait. At its head or SW extremity it receives the Pelorus river from the central mountain-ridge so called.—Also a bay on the Pacific coast of N. America in N.lat. 59°.

ADMIRALTY INLET, an arm of the sea extending between Cockburn island in S lat. 64° 12', W long. 59° 49' and the main land. Its western cape, a high precipitous promontory, has received the name of Cape Gage from its first explorer, Sir James Ross in 1843; its eastern headland, that of Cape Seymour. The SW land, for about 10 m. from Cape Seymour, is formed of deep brown-coloured lava, with a polished surface, grooved in a manner somewhat resembling engine-turning. To the southward the land rises to an elevation of about 2,000 ft. [Ross.]—The same name had been given by Captain Parry, in 1820, to a bay or creek on the S side of Barrow's strait, in N lat. 73° 40', W long. 83°.—It was also given by Vancouver to an extensive inlet from the strait of Juan de Fuca on the coast of New Georgia.

ADMIRALTY ISLAND, a large island on the NW coast of North America, between King George ADM and falls into the NW end of the salt marsh called the Third's archipelago, and the continental or Russio- American shore. Its centre is nearly in N lat. 57° 43', W long. 135° 11'. It is about 180 m. in circuit; its length between 80 and 90 m.; 25 m. broad in the middle; and declines in breadth towards each extremity. The shores, except on the E and SE, are very bold, and afford many convenient bays likely to admit of safe anchorage, with fine streams of fresh water flowing into them. The island is in general moderately elevated; and although it seems composed of a rocky substance, covered with a thin soil, it produces an uninterrupted forest of fine timber trees, chiefly of the pine-tribe, some of which Vancouver found to be 23 ft. in circumference. - Vancouver.

ADMIRALTY ISLANDS, a group of 30 or 40 verdant and beautiful islands in the South Pacific ocean, between the 2d and 3d parallels of S lat., and extending 120 m. from E to W; of which the principal, Great Admiralty island, is from 55 to 60 m. in length, and much larger than the rest. A few of these have been named, apparently by Morello, in 1781, Los Negros, San Gabriel, San Raphael, San- Miguel-el-Horno, Jesus Maria, and La Vendola; which last is the most western. Rocks, reefs, and islets, abound on the N coast of the large island, the southern part of which is yet unknown. All are beautiful, and covered with trees, among which the cocoa-nut is predominant. Most of these islands are inhabited. The natives are a good-looking race of savages. Their colour is black, but not of a deep shade; their hair crisp and black, and frequently reddened with a mixture of ochre and oil. The men use no clothing; the women wear a piece of matting or girdle round the loins. Both sexes wear ear-rings and bracelets of shells; and they chew betel. They are acquainted with the use of iron; but their lances and darts are pointed with a kind of vitreous volcanic lava, ground to a sharp edge on each side, constituting very dangerous weapons. Their canoes are excavated from the trunk of a tree, and carry a square sail of matting, 13 ft. on the side. Captain Carteret took a canoe which was 50 ft. long. Those seen by M. Labillardiere were 32 ft. in length, and only 26 inches wide, the sides being raised with boards. They sail with surprising velocity.—The Admiralty islands were discovered by the Dutchman, Houten, in 1616, who gave them the name of 'The Twenty-five Islands.' Captain Carteret, who visited them in 1767, when the natives are said to have testified hostile dispositions, gave them their present name. They were seen by Morello, a Spanish navigator in 1781, and by the French expedition, in quest of La Perouse, under D'Entrecasteaux, in 1792.
ADMIRALTY PENINSULA, a projection on the W coast of Novaïa Zemlïa, in N lat. 75°, between Glasoff bay and Maschigin bay.

ADMIRALTY RANGE, a magnificent range of mountains, whose peaks, covered with eternal snow, rise to elevations varying from 7,000 to 10,000 ft., near the coast of the great Antarctic continent discovered by Captain Ross in 1841, and by him named Victoria Land.

ADMIRALTY SOUND, a deep inlet, extending 43 m. SE into the western side of Tierra del Fuego. It is 7 m. wide at its entrance, and gradually diminishes to 3. Dawson island fronts its mouth. Mount Hope at its bottom is in S lat. 54° 26', W long 68° 59'.—Beagle Survey.
ADMISTON, a small p. in Dorsetshire. Area 290 acres. Pop. 75.

ADMONT, a town of Styria, on the r. bank of the Enns, 28 m. N of Judenburg, 10 m. E of Lietzen. Pop. 900. "The river here traverses a vast basin, formerly no doubt a lake but now a smiling valley with the wealthy abbey of Admont in its centre, and mighty mountains and picturesque ranges of rocks rising like an amphitheatre around." [Kohl.] The Benedictine abbey of A. was founded in the 12th cent., and its lordship embraces the v. and several others, besides the patronage of 36 churches, 2 gymnasia, and several schools. The edifice itself is comparatively modern and unfinished. It possesses a fine library which Kohl says, was reported to him to contain 100,000 vols.; but Murray's Hand-book for Southern Germany says only 20,000. The villagers find employment in iron-mining and smith work.
ADO, an islet off the coast of Finland, in the gulf of Bothnia, in N lat. 60° 18'.

ADOFOODIAH, a town of W Africa in N lat. 13° 6', E long. (by reckoning) 1° 3'. It is described by Duncan—the only European traveller who has yet visited it—as being as large as Abomey, and as "situated on a dry healthy plain, with a rich red soil, the surface of a sandy nature," and surrounded by a very thick clay wall about 18 ft. high. A merchant whom Mr. Duncan met here said that he had come from Timbuktu in ten days, journeying about 30 m. each day.—Travels in W Africa, in 1845-6.
ADOLFSECK, or ADOLPHSECK, a town of Nassau, on the Aar, a tributary of the Lahn, NW of Wiesbaden.
ADOLFFURT, or ADOLZFURT, a town of Würtemberg, in the circle of the Jaxt, on the Brettach. There are some iron-works here and a powder-mill.
ADOLFSTROEM, a town of Sweden, on the Laiself near Umeä. A silver-mine was at one period wrought here.

ADOLPHUS (Mount), one of the York group of islands in Torres strait, in S lat. 11° 25'. It is a porphyritic rock rising to nearly 500 ft. above the sea, and appears, with the neighbouring islands, to be a submarine prolongation of the great mountainchain of E Australia.—Jukes.
ADOLPHUS (POINT), a cape in King George the Third's archipelago, in N lat. 58° 18', W long. 136° 28′.

ADONI, a district and a town in Hindostan in that portion of the Balaghaut territories, on the S side of the Tombudra, which was ceded by the nizam to the English in 1800. The town of A. is 38 m. N by E from Bellary. It was once a place of considerable strength; but in 1787 was taken and dismantled by Tippoo Saib, and has never recovered its former importance.
ADONY, a town of Hungary, on the r. bank of the Danube, 24 m. E by S of Stuhlweissenburg. Pop. 2,887. There are some Roman remains here. -There are several v. of this name in Hungary.

ADORF, a town of Saxony, in Vogtland, on the Elster, 10 m. SE of Olsnitz. Pop. 2,348 in 1834. Its woollen factories and tanneries were formerly of considerable importance; and it is celebrated for its manufacture of musical instruments.-Also a v. of Switzerland, in the cant. of Thurgau, on the Lützelmurg. Pop. 1,973.—Also a v. of Hanover, NE of Neuenhaus.—Also a v. in the principality of Waldeck, near which are some iron-works.

ADOUR, a considerable river of France which rises in the Pyrenees, at the foot of the Pic d'Espade, 5 m. E of Barèges. Emerging from the chain of the Pyrenees at Grip, 7½ m. above Bagnères de Bigorre, it forms a cataract 100 ft. in height, at the point where the valley of Campan commences. It then flows W and SW through the lower lands of the dep. of the Upper and the Lower Pyrenees, and the fertile part of the Landes, past the towns of Bagnères de Bigorre, Tarbes, Aire, Grenada, Saint-Sever, Dax, and Bayonne; and falls into the bay of Biscay, 3 m. below the last-mentioned town, after a course of 170 | friog, Cardiganshire, 10 m. E by S of Cardigan. # ADP m., of which 57 are from S to N; 70 from E to W; and 43 from NE to SW. It is navigable from Saint- Sever to Bayonne,—a distance of 68 m. The principal tributaries of the A. are, on the right, the Arras, and the Midouze which is navigable; on the left, the Leechez, the Gabas, the Lonts, the Luy, the Gave de Pau, which receives the Gave d'Oléron, the Bidouze, and the Nive. All the last three are navigable. The Nive joins it at Bayonne, that portion of the town called Little Bayonne being situated between it and the A. The country to the r. of the A. is called the Lannes or Landes, and contrasts singularly, in its bleak heathy aspect, with the smiling and fertile tract on the l. The articles of transport on the A. are wines, grain, brandy, timber, resin, and tar.—Louis De Foix, a celebrated engineer who died in the reign of Henry IV., opened a new passage for the A. to the sea, with the design no doubt of destroying a bar which impeded the navigation of the port of Bayonne; but the attempt proved a failure. The sea is constantly forming a bar of sand and gravel, at its mouth, on which there is only 5 or 6 ft. at low water, and the entrance is at all times narrow and difficult. Even in winter, it often happens that 40 or 60 vessels are collected in the lower part of the river unable to get over the bar for months together. The ancient bed of the river is still marked by a number of little lakes. At one time the A. found its way into the sea near the town of Vieux-Boucaut, about 20 m. further N than its present entrance, known as the New Boucaut. During the melting of the snow on the Pyrenees, the current of the A. becomes exceedingly swollen and impetuous.—In the middle of February 1814, the duke of Wellington, having thrown a boat-bridge of 800 yards across the mouth of the river, effected a passage across this river, below Bayonne, in the presence of the French army under Soult.

A'DOWA, the capital of Tigré in Abyssinia, and for some time the residence of the sovereign after the Gallas gained possession of Góndar. It is in N lat. 14° 10′, E long. 39° 4′; 11 m. E of Axum; situated partly on the side and partly at the foot of a hill, on the Hassam, a stream flowing SW to the Tacazzé, and commands a magnificent view of the mountains of Tigré. Ruppell estimates its alt. above sea-level at 6,216 ft. The houses are all of a conical form, regularly disposed into streets or alleys, and interspersed with trees and small gardens. A. contains 800 houses; and, as in Abyssinia each house is calculated to contain at least 10 inhabitants, the population cannot fall short of 8,000. This place is remarkable for an extensive manufacture of cotton cloths, both coarse and fine, which are considered to excel those made in any other part of Abyssinia, and which circulate like money. A. is also the channel by which the communication between the coast and the interior is carried on. The provinces to the S of Adowa abound in cattle and corn, which, with salt, constitute the chief articles of barter. The caravans from Góndar to Massówa, after traversing Wogara, and crossing the Tacazzé, approach A. through the pass of Lamalmon, or by that of Selki, bringing ivory from Walkayt and Shiré, gold from various parts of the interior, and slaves of whom about 1,000 are annually sent through A. to the ports on the Red sea. Lead, tin, copper, Persian carpets, raw silk, velvets, Venetian beads, glass ware, and trinkets are the usual commercial returns for this traffic. The commercial character of this place causes it to be inhabited by a considerable number of Mahommedans, who are the only class of the population at all animated by the spirit of trade.—Bruce.—Salt.—Beke.

ADPAR, a town and borough in the p. of Llandy- Pop. in 1831, 533. It is situated on the Teifi, opposite Newcastle - Emlyn in Carmarthenshire, with which it is connected by a bridge. The principal trade of the town is connected with the numerous cattle-fairs which are held here. As a contributory borough with the hamlet of Emlyn to the Cardigan district of parliamentary burghs, its pop. was returned in 1841 at 1,619; and its electors in 1842-3 at 67.

ADRA, a district of Spain, in the prov. of Granada, the capital of which bears the same name, and stands, with its castle, on the Mediterranean, 45 m. SE of Granada, 8 m. ESE from La Rabida; at the mouth of a river of the same name which descends in a S by W course from the Sierra Nevada. It is in N lat. 36° 44′, W long. 3° 1′. Nearly 3 m. W of A. is the watch-tower of Guarca. In its vicinity are some very productive lead-mines.—Also a river of Turkey in Europe, in Romilia, which joins the Marizza at Adrianople.—Also a town of Syria, NE of Mezárib, called also Edda'árah.

ADRACHA'N, or ADRATCHAN (CAPE), a bold projection on the coast of Karamania, the Mons Phænicus of the ancients. Its summit rises 3,300 ft. above the sea, and presents lofty white cliffs crowned with forests of pines.
ADRAGNO, a town of Sicily, in the Val di Mazzara, 20 m. ENE of Mazzara.

ADRAMYT, or Adhramytti [Turk. Edremi'd,] a town of Natolia, in the sanjak of Karahsí, about 4 m. from the E extremity of the gulf of the same name, and 78 m. N of Smyrna; in N lat. 39° 35', E long. 27° 2'. The town overlooks an extensive valley planted with olives. It is a poor place, but the pop. is estimated by Tanner at 5,000. Wool is exported hence to Constantinople, and galls to Europe. It is about 5 m. NE of the site of the ancient Adramyttíum, or Atramyttéum. Rennell supposes that the gulf and former harbour may have been filled up by alluvion.—The gulf of A. or Edremid has Cape Baba, in N lat. 39° 28', E long. 26° 5', for its NW point, bearing 5½ leagues NE of Cape Sigri in the island of Mitylene. Between these capes lies the N entrance to the gulf, about 5 m. in breadth; and from Cape Sigri to the head of the gulf is 26 m. The SE point of the gulf lies in about N lat. 39° 8'; and between this point and the N point of the gulf of Sandarli, the channel betwixt the mainland and Mitylene averages 8 m. in breadth. The gulf has thus two entrances from the W and from the S. The former is the most frequented; but there is deep water in both.—Admiralty Chart.

ADRASKAND, or ADRUSCUND, a river of Sistan, which rises to the NW of Ghore, and flowing SSW, past Subzawur, is continued in the Heu Rúd river which falls into Lake Hamoon Zerrah.-Dr. Forbes in Geog. Journ. vol. xiv. - Conolly.
ADRETS (Les), a v. of France, in the dep. of Isère, 14 m. NE of Grenoble. Pop. 860. Coal is wrought here.
ADREWA. See ENDERY.

ADRIA, a district and city of Venetian Lombardy, in the prov. of Polesino, at the mouth of the Po, bordering on the legation of Ferrara. The soil of the territory is partly alluvial, partly marshy and sandy, and produces good crops. The town is situated on the Castagnaro, a branch of the Adige, 3 m. N of the Po, and 14 m. from the coast of the gulf to which it gives name, and which once bathed its walls. It is 30 m SSW of Venice, and 15 E of Rovigo; in N lat. 45° 3′, E long. 12° 3′. Pop. 10,000. A. is the see of a bishop, who now, however, generally resides at Rovigo. It exports cattle, grain, silk, linen, and fire-wood.—The ancient A. was a city of the Etrusci. In 430 it was under the Greek emperor. It afterwards to to the service and an expension of the service of the service of the service of the service of the service of the service of the service of the service of the service of the service of the service of the service of the ADR was burnt by the Venetians. In 1529, by the peace of Bologna, it was given up to Venice, and was governed, under the doge, by a patrician with the title of podesta. The relics of the earlier town are a little to the S, but have been gradually buried under successive deposits from the inundations of the Adige and the Po.-Pos. géo. par Daussy.
ADRIAN, a town of Michigan, U.S., opposite the junction of Beaver creek with the Raisin, 67 m. from Detroit, 501 from Washington. Pop. 2,496. A railroad between A. and Toledo was opened in 1836.

ADRIAN (CAPE SAN), a point of land on the NW coast of Spain, the extremity of Mount Boa, SW from Cape Ortegal 50 m. Off this cape is the isle of Cisargas, to pass between which and the mainland good seamanship is required, as there are numerous rocks, and the tide sets strongly eastwards. - There is a headland on the W coast of Majorca of the same name.

ADRIANO, a river of Sicily, flowing SE to the Gibello, which it joins opposite to Bronte. ADRIANO'PLE [Turk. EDRENEH], a city, formerly the European capital of the Turkish empire, in the prov. of Romelia, in N lat. 41° 41', and E long. 26° 36' [Struve], 135 m. NW by W of Constantinople. It was erected in honour of Antinous, and originally bore the name of Antinoa; that of Adrianople [Adrianopolis] it takes from the emperor Adrian, by whom it was rebuilt or restored, and made the capital of the province of Hæmimontana. A. is situated on the sides and at the base of a low hill, or rather on "one low hill with three eminences" [Blount], on the E bank of the Tundsha, near its junction with the Arda and the Maritza, "in one of the most fertile plains in the world." It is about 5 m. in circumference; but is not surrounded by a wall flanked with towers, as stated in some authorities. Its effect from a distance is agreeable, being "thickly studded with trees, which contrast beautifully with the mosques, minarets, and various colours of the habitations." [Keppel.] It is also surrounded on all sides by mulberry-plantations. It contains no fewer than forty mosques, and is well-provided with baths. The houses are substantially built, but the streets are narrow, irregular, dirty, and, in some instances, "darkened by wooden projections from the opposite houses." [Macmichael.] These projections, which are the eaves of the shelving roofs, are, however, a mode of structure not without its use as affording protection from the heat of the sun. The chief objects of attention in A. are the mosque of Selim, and the bazaar of Ali Pacha. But the general aspect of the city is now that of desertion and desolation; and its original splendour is only to be traced in its ruins. It is supplied with water by means of the aqueduct of Soliman. The harbour of Enos, situated at the mouth of the Hebrus-which, except during the dry season, between May and autumn, is navigable for long flat vessels of 250 tons as far as A .-- affords important facilities to the commerce of this city. Its tanneries and dressed leather are important; and it has manufactories of silk, woollen, cotton, distilleries of rose-water and other perfumes, and dye-works. Its principal exports are fine wool, raw silk, the yellow dye-stuff known under the name of grains d'Avignon, here called the Rutchuk berry, wine, wax, and leather. A. is the depôt of Romelia for all goods that come to Constantinople from England, France, and Austria. The adjacent country is very fertile, and its red wine is esteemed the best in Turkey.—In the neighbourhood of A., Constantine defeated Licinius in 323; and in 378 Valens was defeated by the Goths, though the walls of A. repelled the subsequent attack of the flushed victors. In 1360 A. was taken from the Greeks by Amurath I., and formed part of the exarchate of Ravenna, and in 1482 | since that period it continued the occasional seat of government; and it became the favourite residence of his successors, Achmet III., Mahommed IV., and Mustafa. In 1754, and again in 1788, it suffered severely from fire. In 1829 the Russian army under Diebitsch, having crossed the Balcan, advanced upon A. by Janboli, and was permitted to enter the city without opposition on the 20th of August; and on the 14th of September a treaty was concluded here between Russia and Turkey, by which new political frontiers were given to Asia; the government of Moldavia, Wallachia, and Servia, placed upon a new footing; and the independence of Greece formally acknowledged by the Porte. A. is the seat of a Greek bishop under the patriarch of Constantinople, and the residence of a British consul. The population was estimated by Dr. Macmichael, in 1817-18, at 90,000; by Major Keppel, in 1829-30, at the same number; by Balbi at 100,000; and by some recent authorities at 130,000, of whom 30,000 are Greeks. Major Keppel says that the houses in 1829-30 amounted in number to 23,000, of which 15,000 were Turkish.—Blount's Voyage into the Levant, 1634.— Pococke's Travels.—Macmichael's Journey from Moscow to Constantinople.—Keppel's Journey across the Balcan. -Slade's Records of Travel in 1829-31.—Encyclopédie Moderne.

ADRIAN'S WALL, a celebrated Roman work in the north of England. This work, though called by the Roman historians murus—which signifies a wall of stone—was only composed of earth covered with green turf. It was carried from the Solway frith, in as direct a line as possible, to the river Tyne on the E, at the place where the town of Newcastle now stands; so that it must have been above 60 English, and nearly 70 Roman, miles in length. It consisted of four parts: 1, the principal agger, mound of earth or rampart, on the brink of the ditch; 2, the ditch, on the N side of the rampart; 3, another rampart on the S side of the principal one, about five paces distant from it; 4, a large rampart on the N side of the ditch. For many ages, this work has been in so ruinous a condition that it is impossible to discover its original dimensions with certainty. But from their appearance, it seems probable that the principal rampart was at least 10 or 12 ft. high, and the S one not much less; the northern one was considerably lower. The ditch, taken as it passes through a limestone quarry near Harlow-hill, appears to have been 9 ft. deep and 11 ft. wide at the top. The N rampart was about 20 ft. distant from the ditch.

ADRIATIC SEA, a great arm of the Mediterranean, extending SE and NW 465 geog. m., between the E coast of Italy, and the shores of Illyria, Croatia, Dalmatia, and Albania. By some geographers the name of this sea has been extended to the southern extremity of Greece. In the present article we shall regard the entrance of this gulf as lying between Cape Santa Maria di Leuca, on the Italian side, in N lat. 39° 48', E long. 18° 23', and Cape Drasti, the NW point of Corfu, in N lat. 39° 48', E long. 19° 38'. The level of the A. is the same as that of the Mediterranean; and its tidal action almost equally imperceptible. Its superficial area is estimated at 62,156 sq. m. [Berghaus.] Perhaps 42,000 geog. square m. is nearer the truth. The western or Italian shores of the A. are in general low and washed by a deep sea. Towards the head of the gulf they continue low, and are marshy and shoally. The eastern shores are bold and rocky, and sprinkled with islets and shoals. It would appear that the whole upper, or NW end of the A. is gradually shallowing; and the rate at which the land is here gaining upon the sea may be estimated from the fact that while, in the 12th century, Adria from the S 5 or 6 ft.—The gulf of Trieste (Tergestinus was 9,000 or 10,000 metres from the shore, it is now nearly 33,000. Ravenna, once celebrated as a port, is now 5 m. inland; and the lagunes of Venice are rapidly filling up. [Cuvier sur les Rev. du Globe.] "From the northernmost point of the gulf of Trieste, where the Isonzo enters, down to the S of Ravenna, there is an uninterrupted series of recent accessions of land, more than 100 m. in length, which, within the last 2,000 years, have increased from 10 to 20 m. in breadth." [Lyell.] It is, however, alleged, that the A. has encroached on the coasts of Dalmatia and Istria, while retiring from its western and northern shores. [Tuckey.] The deposits consist partly of mud, partly of calcareous rock, showing complete identity of composition with the strata of the Sub- Apennine hills. During the melting of the snows in spring, the Po and the Adige render the waters at the head of the gulf almost brackish. The currents of the A. are very irregular, but there appears to be a prevailing northerly current entering the gulf on the Albanian side, and running along the eastern shores, and a south-easterly current on the west. The gales here called boras, coming from ENE or NNE, are dangerous in winter on the leeward shores of the A. They occur most frequently and violently in the gulf of Quarnero, and generally last three days. The principal coasting-trade is carried on by Austrian vessels, and a few belonging to the States-of-the-Church. It has been proposed to open a navigable communication between the A. and the Mediterranean, across the States-of-the- Church, by means of a canal which, commencing on the A. side at Ancona, will run SW by S to near Fuligno, whence it will send off a branch, by the SE side of the lake of Thrasimene, to Florence and Pisa, while another branch will run SW to the Tiber; but the proposal has not yet been carried into effect. [Pietro Ferrari: Dell'apertura di un Canale, &c. Roma: 1825.] Name. It has been much questioned whether it is to the town of Adria in Venetian-Lombardy, or to that of Atri (Adria Picena) in Abruzzo Ulteriore Primo that the Adriatic owes its name. Strabo and Pliny assign this honour to the former; but opposed to them are the authorities of an ancient historian cited by Tzetzes, a scholiast on Lycophron, of Aurelius Victor, of Paul the Deacon [Hist. Longob. ii. 19], and a number of medals of the highest antiquity. [See C. Orlandi, Delle citta d'Italia. T. ii. p. 278.] Greek classic writers call this sea the Ionian bay or gulf. By Latin writers it is called Hadria, or Mare Superum. The A. is very commonly known under the appellation of the Gulf of Venice, from the city of that name which once claimed and exercised exclusive dominion over this sea, and whose doge annually wedded it, on Ascension day, by the ceremony of throwing a ring into its waters with great pomp. Gulfs.] The Adriatic contains several important gulfs. On the coast of the kingdom of Naples is the large gulf or bay of Manfredonia (Sinus Urias), lying between the parallels of 41° 19' and 41° 48'. This is the only place on the Italian side where a ship will find protection from the northerly gales. With this gulf the Laguna di Salpi (Palus Salupina) and the smaller lagune called the Pantano Salso are connected.—Towards the head of the A. is the gulf of Venice, extending from the delta of the Po, or the Punta della Maestra, in N lat. 44° 59', E long. 12° 32', to the mouth of the Tagliamento in N lat. 45° 39', E long. 13° 6'. The tide is more perceptible towards the head of the A. than in any other part of the Mediterranean; and near Venice the common rise of spring-tides is 3 ft., and with a strong wind the Tagliamento and Point Salone in N lat. 45° 30', E long. 13° 34', is about 25 m. in depth. — The peninsula of Istria separates the gulf of Trieste from that of Quarnero. This latter gulf, called by Pliny, Flanaticus sinus, by Orosius, Liburnicus, and by Pomponius Mela, Polaticus, is a singularly curious and intricate arm, containing four large islands, and innumerable islets and rocks, and communicating with the A. by four very dangerous passages.—On the Dalmatian coast, are the gulf of Narenta, on the N of the peninsula of Sabioncello, intersected by the 43d parallel; and the gulf of Cattaro, an irregular inlet, consisting of two large basins, the first anciently named Sinus Rhyzonicus, opening in N lat. 42° 23', E long. 18° 32'.—On the Dalmatian coast, the gulf or bay of Drino lies between the mouth of the Drino and Cape Rodoni in N lat. 41° 38', E long. 19° 27'; Durazzo bay lies 20 m. farther S; and the beautiful gulf of Avlona 40 m. to the S of that of Durazzo, having its entrance in N lat. 40° 30'. Basin and Rivers.] The Apennines and the Alps send numerous streams to this sea. The rivers discharging themselves into the A. which belong to the kingdom of Naples are: the Ofanto (Aufidus) which flows into the A. 4 m. NW of Barletta; the Candelaro, a river of the Capitanata, which reaches the sea 4 m. SW of Manfredonia; the Fortore or Fronto; the Tiferno or Biferno, which throws itself into the A. to the SE of Termoli; the Trigno (Trinum portuosum); the Sangro (Sagrus); the Aterno or Pescara; the Vomano, the mouth of which is 6 m. NE of Atri; and the Tronto, which has its mouth in N lat. 42° 54'.— In the States-of-the-Church are the Chienti (Flusor), which terminates its course to the SE of Civita Nuova; the Potenza, which discharges itself at Porto Recanati; the Musone, which falls into the sea to the NE of Loretto; the Esina; the Cesano; the Metauro, which has its mouth 6 m. SW of Fano; the Foglia, (Pisaurus) whose estuary is at Pesaro; the Marecchia, which passes Rimini; the Ronco or Bidente; the Montone, which reaches the A. 4 m. NE of Ravenna. the Lamone, which passes near Faenza; the Reno, a tributary of the Po di Primaro, which crosses the marshes of Comacchio, and whose mouth is in N lat. 44° 35'.—The vast lagunes of Comacchio, which present the appearance of a number of floating isles, are terminated on the N by the Po of Volano, a canal which begins near Ferrara and joins the canal of Ceuto or Postello di Ferrara.—The Pô (Padus, Eridanus) which separates the kingdom of Lombardian-Venice from the States - of - the - Church, the largest river of Italy, at Serravalle divides into two main branches, the Pô Maëstro and the Pô di Goro, which at their outfall into the A. are nearly 12 m. apart. The alluvial matter carried down by the Po and its numerous branches has formed many shoals on this part of the coast.—The other great tributaries of the Adriatic on this side are the Adige which falls into the sea at Porto-Fossone; the Brenta which flows into the port of Brondolo; the Piave which discharges itself by two mouths into the sea; the Livenza; and the Tagliamento which forms part of the boundary of the provinces of Udine and Venice.—On the Illyrian coast is the Isonzo (Sontius) which to the NE of Aquila divides into two branches,—the Isonzato, and the Sdobba,— which form the isle of Morosina, and fall together into the gulf of Trieste. The small but interesting river of Timave likewise flows into this gulf.—On the eastern side of the A. there are the estuaries of several important rivers. The Cettina flows into the sea under the walls of Almissa. The Narenta at Opus divides into three branches, all of Sinus) which opens to the SW, between the mouth of carries the waters of the lake of Scutari to the A. The Drin, or Drino, formed by the junction of the White and Black Drin, falls into the gulf of Drino. To the S of the Drino are the Scombi (Pangasus); the Beratino or Ergent coming from Romelia, and the Voïoussa, the ancient Aoüs, which throws itself into the sea under the ruins of the celebrated Apollonia. Islands.] The Adriatic, on its eastern side, is remarkable for its numerous islands. On the coast of Naples, in N lat. 42° 7', are the four islands of Tremiti, the ancient Diomedeæ Insulæ. These are the only important islands on the eastern coast of Italy.—The E coast of the A. from Istria to Ragusa is lined by islands of varying dimensions, which all appear to have been torn from the adjoining continent by some violent convulsion of nature.—Among the Illyrian isles are the Brioni to the NW of Pola, and the numerous isles of the gulf of Quarnero, the largest of which is Cherzo or Cherso the ancient Crispa, or Crexa, separated from the continent by the canal of Farisina, and connected by a bridge with the island of Ozero. To the SW of A. are the islands of Sansego and Unie. The large isle of Veglia to the N of Cherso is separated from the continent on the E by the Morlacca channel. In the southern part of the gulf of Quarnero are the Dalmatian isles of Arbe or Barbado; Parvi Chio between Veglia and Arbe; San Gregorio or Drivenico, to the N and near the island of Arbe; Goli, likewise to the NE of that isle; Pago, separated on the N from Arbe by the canal of Pago; Maon, Ulbo, Premuda, Melida, inhabited only by fishermen; Cononata, separated from the continent by the canal of Mezzo, and connected, according to some geographers, with the island of Grossa or Lunga; and Ugliano opposite the town of that name and to the NW of the isle of Pasman, with which it forms the eastern side of the channel of Zara. Among the numerous islands which belong to the circle of Spalatro are Bua, united by a causeway to the town of Trau; Brazza, a large island separated from the continent by a channei from 3 to 5 m. broad; Solita to the NW of Brazza; Lesina (Pharos) between Brazza on the N and Curzola on the S; which latter island lies to the NW of the peninsula of Sabioncello from which it is separated by the gulf of Narenta; Lissa, 10 m. W of Lesina; and San Andrea and some smaller islets to the W of Lissa. In the circle of Ragusa, are Curzola (Corcyra nigra) separated by a narrow canal from the peninsula of Sabioncella; Torcello situated about the middle of this channel; Lagosta surrounded by small islands and rocks; Meleda separated by a channel of the same name from the peninsula of Sabioncello; Giupana or Seipan, between Meleda and Ragusa, one of the islands which the ancients named Elaphites; and the island of San- Nicolo, belonging to the circle of Cattaro and situated at the entrance of the port of Badua. Ports.] We shall now briefly notice the safest and most frequented ports of the Adriatic. On the SE coast of Italy, opening from a canal of the same name, is the port of Otranto, the ancient Hydruntum. About 35 m. further to the NW is the bay of Brindisi, the famous Brundusium of the ancients, and once the best harbour on the W side of the A. At a distance of 60 m. from Brindisi is the little port of Bari; 30 m. NW from which is that of Barletta. At the distance of 26 m. NNW from Barletta is the bay of Manfredonia, with an extent of coast of 17 m., and a depth of from 9 to 14 fathoms on a clear sandy bottom. The port of Ancona in N lat. 43° 38', E long. 13° 31', is the most commercial on the E coast of Italy, and here vessels may ride in 7 which flow into the canal of Narenta. The Boïana or 8 fathoms at a short cable's length from the shore. The ancient port of Rimini (Ariminum) at the mouth of the Marecchia, is now rendered inaccessible by banks of alluvial deposit, like that of Ravenna also in which Augustus kept the fleets of the Adriatic. From the port of Goro, at the mouth of the southernmost arm of the Po, to that of Chioggia, the coast consists of low flat land formed by the alluvial deposits of that river and of the Adige, and intersected by numerous streams. From Chioggia to the port of Malamocco, a low, narrow island named Lido, separates the lagunes from the sea. Malamocco, one of the three entrances to the lagunes, is the largest and most important of the ports of Venice. To the N of this island is the port of San-Nicolo di Lido, the entrance to the lagunes nearest Venice. These lagunes are marshes or extensive ponds, formed by the waters of the Po, the Adige, the Brenta and the Piave. From the low land which these have covered rise the numerous small islands on which the city is situated. Beyond the mouth of the Tagliamento the lagunes of Marano and Grado extend E a distance of 7 m. The port of Trieste, the ancient Tergeste, founded in 1750 by the empress Maria Theresa and erected by her into a free port, is the centre of the maritime commerce of the empire of Austria. It has a mole harbour; and about a mile to the S there is the bay of Muggia which affords excellent anchorage. Pola, at the bottom of a small gulf, 8 m. to the N of Punta di Promontore, the southernmost point of Istria, is resorted to chiefly by the tunny fishers. The port of Fiume is the principal harbour on the coast of Croatia; but there are numerous small harbours on the various islands in the gulf of Quarnero, and to the south of it. On the coast of Dalmatia is the spacious port of Zara which extends to the N of the town and is sheltered by it from the S; the excellent port of Sebenico at the mouth of the Kerka; the little port of Trau; the great bay of Salona; the large port of Spalatro, that of Santa Croce or Grayoso, opening from the bay of Ombla Finmera, which is the true port of Ragusa; the beautiful and cheerful port of Cattaro; and the small harbour of Budua in N lat. 42° 16'.— On the coast of Albania are the ports of Antivari, Dulcigno, the mouth of the Drino, Durazzo (Epidaurus, afterwards Dyrrachium) and Valona, which is protected by the island Sasseno. ADRIEN (St.) a v. and commune of France, in the dep. of Côtes-du-Nord. Pop. 771.
ADRIERES, a commune and t. of France, in the dep. of Vienne, 15 m. SE of Montmorillon. Pop. 1,400.
ADRIGATE. See ATEGERAT.
ADRIGOOL, or ADDERGOOLE, a p. in co. Mayo. Area 36,630 acres. Pop. 7,379, of whom not above 100 are Protestants.—Also a p. in co. Galway. Area 8,442 acres. Pop. 3,321.
ADSJAR, a town of Arabia, on the Persian gulf, 80 m. S of El-Khatif, in N lat 25° 8′, E long. 50°.
ADSTOCK, a p. and v. in Buckinghamshire. Area of p. 1,130 acres. Pop. 419. The v. is 3 m. NW of Winslow.
ADSTONE, a v. in Northamptonshire, <math>6\frac{3}{4}</math> m. WNW of Towcester. Pop. 166.
ADU', a name sometimes given to that portion of the coast of Guinea which lies between capes Lahú and Asiné. It is sometimes called the Kwakwa coast.

ADULA, a name sometimes given to that portion of the Rhætian Alps which stretches from the St. Gothard to the Moschelhorn and the Bernardine, in the Grisons, and may be regarded as one of the most elevated tracts of country in Europe. The pass on the E side of the Bernardine has an elevation of 7,115 ft.
ADULA, or ADALA, a town of Natolia, on the r. ÆG bank of the Kadeshai or Hermus, 30 m. SE by S of Akhissar; 9 hours from Sardes.
ADULAR, a mountain of Spain, in Navarre, between Pampeluna and St. Jean Pied de Port in France, on the N side of the plain of Roncesvalles. It is sometimes called Mount Altobiscar.

ADULIS, or ADU'LI, a ruined town of Abyssinia, in N lat. 15° 16', a little to the NE of Afté. There seems little doubt that this is the Adulis of Arrian. The ruins lie, says Ruppell, on the N side of the bed of a river flowing from the SW, and consist of small unhewn fragments of lava.
ADUR, a river of Sussex, falling into Shoreham bay, after a southerly course of about 25 m. It is navigable to Binesbridge.

ADVENTURE BAY, a large bay on the coast of Bruny island off Van Diemen's Land, between Fluted cape in S lat. 43° 22' and Cape Frederick Henry in 43° 13'. It is well sheltered, and affords good anchorage, with abundance of fresh water.
ADVENTURE ISLAND, a low islet in the Pacific, in S lat. 17° 5', W long. 144° 14'.—Raper.
ADVENTURE ISLETS, three small islets, near the E point of Palmer's land, which is in S lat. 63° 25', W long. 57° 55'.— Willis.
ADWALTON, a hamlet in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 4 m. SE by S from Bradford.
ADWELL, a p. in Oxfordshire, 2 m. SE from Tetsworth. Area 500 acres. Pop. 46.
ADWICK-ON-DEARNE, a v. and p. in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 631 m. NNE of Rotherham, near the North Midland railway. Area 1,130 acres. Pop. 180.
ADWICK-LE-STREET, a p. and v. in the W. R. of Yorkshire. Area 2,950 acres. Pop. 554.
ADZANETA, a town of Valencia, 16 m. NW by N of Oropesa.
ADZEL, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Livonia, on the Aa, 25 m. SW of Valk.
ADZERBALLIG, a v. of Denmark, in the island of Alsen, 8 m. NE of Sonderburg.
ADZUL, a town of Wallachia, on the Jalonitza, near its junction with the Danube.
ÆDELFORS. See ADELFORS.
ÆDLHOLZEN. See ADELHOLZEN.

ÆGADES, or ÆGADEAN ISLANDS, a cluster of islands, the Insulæ Ægusæ of the ancients, lying off the W coast of Sicily. They consist of Maritimo, the ancient Hiera, 21 m. distant from the nearest point of Sicily; Favignana, the ancient Ægusa, 10 m. SE by E of Maritimo; Levanso, the ancient Phorbantia, 4 m. NNE of Favignana; and the Formiche, or Ants, two low barren islets off the SE point of Levanso. These islands are all noticed in our pages under their several heads.—Smyth.

ÆGE'AN SEA, the name anciently given to that portion of the Mediterranean now commonly called the Grecian archipelago, or the Archipelago, extending from the E end of the Mediterranean to the Dardanelles.
ÆGELSTAWIK, a small port of Sweden, in Sudermannland, near the town of Sodertledge.
ÆGERI, a small lake of Switzerland, in the SE part of the canton of Zug, which discharges its waters by the Lorze into the lake of Zug below Baar.

ÆGIDY (St.), a town in the archd. of Austria, in the circle Ober dem Wienerwald, on the Trasen, 26 m. S of Saint-Poelten. Lat. 47° 52' N. ÆGI'NA, EGINA, or ENGINA, an island of Greece, in the middle of the gulf of the same name. It is about 20 m. in circumference; and its NE extremity is 11 m. SSW from Athens. Its soil is stony, and in some quarters its surface is rugged; but it is well-cultivated, especially its western half, and produces a considerable quantity of grain, besides wine, olives, almonds, and figs. Mount St. Elias, or Oros, occupies the S part of the island. Near the NE extremity the rugged ridge of Panhellenium, bears on one of its eminences, amidst a forest of pines, the ruins of the ancient temple of Jupiter Panhellenius, and looks down upon Cape Colonna, Salamis, the Parthenon, and Eleusis. Count Pecchio estimates the pop. at 10,000. It is probably considerably less: some estimate it at only 4,000. The modern town or rather village of Æ. is situated on a pointed hill towards the N coast, separated by a narrow valley from the hill on which the temple of Jupiter stands.—Leake.—Pecchio.

ÆGINA (GULF OF), a gulf on the eastern coast of Greece, the Saronicus Sinus of the ancients, of which the SW extremity is formed by Cape Skylleo or Skyllo, in N lat. 37° 27', E long. 23° 33'; and the opposite extremity by Cape Colonna, or Sunium, in N lat. 37° 39', E long. 24° 1'.
AELBEEK, a v. and commune of Belgium, in the prov. of W Flanders, 4 m. SSW of Courtray. Pop. 1,831.
AELST. See Aalst.
AELTEN. See AALTEN.
AELTRE. See AALTERE.
AENG, a town of Arracan, on a river of the same name which flows into the bay of Bengal in N lat. 19° 55′, E long. 93° 45′, 66 m. SE from Arracan.
AENHOLT. See ANHOLT. ÆNOS (MOUNT), MONTE NERO, or the BLACK

MOUNTAIN, a mountain in Cephalonia, and the highest in the Ionian islands, having an altitude of 5,306 ft. [Slater.] A large portion of it is every winter covered with snow. [Dr. John Davy.] It commands an exquisite sea-view of the whole of Cephalonia, Ithaca, and Santa Maura, with Acarnania, Mount Pindus, and the Arcadian mountains in the distance.
ÆOLIAN ISLANDS. See LIPARI.
AERNEN, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. of Valais, near the l. bank of the Rhone, 12 m. NE of Brieg.
AEROE. See ARROE.
AERON. See ABERAERON.
AERSCHOT, a fortified town of S. Brabant, on the l. bank of the Demer, 18 m. NE from Brussels, 4 m. W from Montaigu. Pop. in 1829, 3,615. There are extensive breweries and distilleries here.
AERTRYKE, a town of W Flanders, 10 m. SSW of Bruges. Pop. 2,176.
AERZBERG, a mountain of Styria, 21 m. NW of Bruck. Alt. 2,560 ft. Lat. 47° 30' N.
AERZEELE, a town of W Flanders, 4 m. E of Thielt. Pop. 3,192.
AERZEN, a town of Hanover, on the Griese. Pop. 1,023.
AESCH, a town of Switzerland, in the canton of Basle, 5 m. S of Basle. Pop. 814.
AESCHI, a v. of Switzerland, in the canton of Berne, on the SW side of the lake of Thun. Pop. 619.—Also a v. on the coast of Soleure, 6 m. W of Soleure. Pop. 270.

ESTUARY, or ESTUARY, a geographical term, from the Latin æstuarium, usually applied to that portion of the mouth of a river in which the tide rises and falls within a wider channel than properly belongs to the bed of the river itself. It differs from an inlet, or small arm of the sea, inasmuch as it has a river flowing into it, by the action of which, as well as of the tides, the general outline of its coasts has been modified. The word frith or firth, as used on the coasts of Scotland, appears to be synonymous with this term,
AETH. See ATH.

ÆTNA, a mountain of Sicily, the most celebrated and indeed the most remarkable of the mountains of Europe,-a furious volcano which has often spread terror and devastation over the surrounding country. Ætna, now called by the Sicilians Monte Gibello, or Mongibello, is situated on the NE side of the island, between Taormina and Catania, in N lat. 37° 43', E long. 15°, in the Val di Demone, SSW of Messina; NNW of Catania. Various conjectures have been formed respecting the origin of the name Ætna. In itineraries it is usually written Æthana: hence some trace its origin to the Greek aithein, 'to burn;' or the Hebrew athana, 'a furnace,' or ætana, 'darkness.' Humboldt conjectures it may have been so named by the ancient Siculi. Whatever may have been the origin of the name, the mountain itself has for many ages attracted the notice of mankind. Its immense size and solitary elevation,-the beauty and magnificence of the surrounding scenery,-the terrific grandeur of the convulsions and changes to which it has been subject,-have all, and each in their turn, excited the descriptive powers of the poet, and afforded matter of interesting research to the philosopher. Pindar calls it 'the Pillar of Heaven.' Here, according to ancient mythology, were erected the forges and workshops of the Cyclops. The appearance of Ætna, when viewed at a distance, is that of an obtuse truncated cone, extended at the base, and terminating in a bifurcated vertex or top, that is in two eminences at a considerable distance from each other. It is in general of a symmetrical conical form, but its larger diameter runs E and W. The accompanying cut, No. 1, represents Æ. as seen from near Charybdis; No. 2, from Catania. Fig. 1. Fig. 2. Upon a nearer approach, the traveller is surprised and astonished at the wild and grotesque appearance of the whole mass. Scattered over the immense declivity of Æ., but especially in its lower regions, he beholds a great number of small conical hills gently rising from its surface to the height of 400 or 500 ft., and covered with rich verdure. These are all secondary cones which have been formed by lateral eruptions of the mountain which are much more frequent than those from the top [Dolomieu]: the lava finding less resistance in piercing the flanks than in rising to the summit of so lofty a mountain. Dr. Daubeny says these minor cones form the most striking and original feature in the physiognomy of Æ. Beyond these, on the skirts and flanks of the vast mountain, appear villages, hamlets, and monasteries, in thick profusion, —a population of nearly 150,000, diffused through 77 towns and villages, dwelling in this fair but dangerous region, which yields the finest corn, oil, wine, and fruit in Sicily. As his eye ascends, he discovers an immense forest of oaks and pines girdling the mountain on every side, and forming a beautiful zone of green round its middle; above this appears the hoary head of the mountain itself, boldly projecting into the clouds, and covered with eternal snow. From these general characters, so deeply and distinctly impressed upon the different parts of its surface, Æ. has been divided into three regions: the lower or fertile region,-the middle or woody region,-and the superior or snowy region. These three regions are equally distinguished by their temperature. The traveller, faint and oppressed by the sultry heat of the lower region, escapes with delight into the shade of the woods where the air is cool and refreshing; but, upon emerging from the forest, the temperature rapidly declines, and to all the difficulties and dangers attending his journey to the summit are now added the inconveniences arising from the piercing coldness of the air, and the chilling blasts from the S to which these higher regions are exposed. Hence another source of distinction has arisen; and these divisions, affording an epitome of the different climates throughout the world, have been respectively denominated the torrid, temperate, and frigid zones of Æ. The first and second zones afford the means of subsistence to 180,000 inhabitants. The elevation of Æ. has been carefully and approximately ascertained by the successive measurements of Saussure, Sir George Shuckburgh, and Captain Smyth; by the first barometrically, and by the two latter both barometrically and geometrically, as shown in the following table:- Eng. feet. Saussure, . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 10,874 Smyth, THE RESERVE TO STREET, CAR The greatest difference in these admeasurements being only 89 ft., such a near approximation gives confidence in the result; but, when it is added, that Sig. Cacciatore, the present astronomer royal at Palermo, Sir John Herschell and Captain Smyth, have all by measurements, entirely independent of each other, fixed it within a fathom of 10,874 ft., we must feel satisfied that the exact altitude of Æ. has been as nearly ascertained as that of any other celebrated mountain in the world. The following elevations are from Smyth:- Base of the cone of Ætna, . . . . 9,797
English lodge, near the foot of the cone, . 9,596 Philosopher's tower, . . . 9,489 Admitting the height of the mountain as above ascertained, the visual ray from its most elevated point will be 130 m., a fact fully accordant with the testimony of the Maltese. Malta is seen from its summit, a small speck in the edge of the horizon; from Malta A. is not visible but in the most favourably reflective state of the atmosphere. In the direction of Africa, the most distant object visible is the rocky mountainous island of Pantelaria, 180 m. off: but this is the case only at sunrise, when the atmospherical refraction is greatest, and when the shadow of Æ. is nearly in a line with that island. As the diurnal altitude of the sun increases, the shadow of the mountain diminishes proportionally with the refraction, and the island or its image disappears gradually till it is no longer visible. The diameter of the visible horizon is only 260 m. from the summit of Æ., according to the law of vision as regulated by the globularity of this earth, and the elevation of the place of observation. Near the sea, Æ. rises at an angle of between 2° and 3°; the inclination afterwards increases to 5°, and towards mid-height to as much as 15°. The proper cone rises at an angle of from 13° to 15°; and the acclivity of the last cone is from 30° to 39°. The base of Æ. forms an ellipse whose major axis inclines more inland than towards the sea. The circumference of the base of the mountainous region of Ætna, as defined by the rivers Simetto and Alcantara, and on the S and SE by the sea, is nearly 90 m.; but its lava-streams have extended over a much greater space, some of them exceeding 30 m. in extent [Spallanzani]. Hence it is sometimes represented as embracing a circumference of 180 m. [Philipps' Geology, in which Mr. P. follows Spallanzani]. On the SE side the plain of Catania reaches to the foot of the mountain, and declines imperceptibly to the sea. The geological structure of Æ. is of the tertiary period; and it consists partly of volcanic, partly of sedimentary rocks.
